01 / Identity

Ryan Cheong — SUTD undergrad on the Power Electronics track. Trained in Aerospace Electronics at Temasek Polytechnic, now building things that move, lift, and drive.

02 / Practice

From 8-layer PCBs at Rolls-Royce to a discrete H-bridge built from scratch to lift elderly users off a seat — hands-on from schematic to final build.

03 / Now

Building the Liftoff Chair for sarcopenia assistance and leading the EVAM 1.0 project at SUTD EV Club. Open to internships in power electronics & embedded hardware.

01 / About the engineer [ PROFILE.DAT ]

Trained in Aerospace Electronics at Temasek Polytechnic, now on the Power Electronics track at SUTD. I build hardware — from schematic to breadboard to PCB, and back to the bench when it doesn't work the first time.

I've prototyped an 8-layer PCB with MOSFETs in TO-247-4 packages at Rolls-Royce's aerospace R&D lab, built a discrete MOSFET H-bridge from scratch to drive a lifting chair for the elderly, welded a solar dehydrator chassis that reaches 50 °C, and put together a variable bench PSU from empty sheet metal. I believe good engineering feels quiet — clean rails, honest grounds, deliberate choices.

Outside class I'm leading the EVAM 1.0 restoration at SUTD EV Club, writing Telegram bots that babysit 3D printers, and occasionally taking home a hackathon trophy.

CASE STUDY / ON THE BENCH / Q2 2026

Liftoff Chair —
an H-bridge built to
preserve dignity.

▸ DOMAINAssistive Power Electronics

▸ HARDWAREDiscrete H-Bridge, IRF4905 / IRF520

▸ PARAMETERS24V DC Rail | 15A Operational Peak Peak

▸ TOPOLOGY555 Timer Analog Edge Logic (Zero-IoT)

▸ TARGETEfficiency > 93% | Isolated Grounds

▸ STATUSSchematic Validated & Bench Prototyped

Sarcopenia takes legs from people slowly. For my ongoing design project, I'm building a chair that assists standing — strong enough to push someone up without injuring them, subtle enough to let them keep their dignity.

The design is deliberately offline. No cloud, no smartphone app, no firmware update tomorrow that breaks it. A 555 timer, a ripple counter, a flip-flop, and a discrete MOSFET H-bridge driving a linear actuator. Analogue logic that can't be bricked.

◉ ALTIUM DESIGNER · SHEET2.SCHDOC · REV B
REV. B · 2026.04.15 555 → 74LS93N → 74LS74AN → HF115F → IRF4905/IRF520 H-BRIDGE → LINEAR ACTUATOR A4 · DRAWN BY R.CHEONG

▸ CONTROL LAYER

The timing brain

A NE555P generates a clean clock that feeds an SN74LS93N ripple counter, whose outputs gate an SN74LS74AN D flip-flop — the state latch deciding lift vs. lower.

  • 555 · CLK generation
  • 74LS93 · 4-bit counter
  • 74LS74 · SR latch
  • MSP103C04 · top / bottom limit switches

▸ DRIVE LAYER

The H-bridge from scratch

Four discrete MOSFETs in classic H-bridge topology — IRF4905 PMOS high-side and IRF520 NMOS low-side — with P2N2222A BJT level-shifters translating logic-level drive into the 12 V rail.

  • PMOS_L / PMOS_R · high-side switches
  • NMOS_L / NMOS_R · low-side switches
  • 1N5408 diodes · flyback protection
  • HF115F relay · direction latch

▸ OUTPUT

A linear lift

The H-bridge drives a 12 V linear actuator beneath the seat — bidirectional, current-limited by the gate resistors, mechanically constrained by the limit switches that feed back into the control layer.

  • 12 V DC rail · single supply
  • Linear actuator · seat elevation
  • Analogue feedback · no firmware
  • Start button · single-press dignified

▸ VALIDATION LOOP

From Altium to Falstad, before anything burns.

Rather than commit the design to copper blind, I rebuilt the entire topology inside Falstad's real-time circuit simulator — watching current flow through the H-bridge node by node, verifying gate-drive timing, and confirming the flip-flop latches direction correctly before the actuator sees a single amp.

Every revision in Altium is mirrored in Falstad. It takes longer, but nothing mysterious shows up on the bench.

◉ BENCH · CUSTOM PSU · HAND-BUILT

Singapore College of Insurance

Temp Staff · Content & Documentation
Made tutorial videos using Colossyan and handled official exam documentation for professional certification programmes.

2025 · PRESENT

SUTD Electric Vehicle Club

EXCO · EVAM 1.0 Project Lead
Leading the EVAM 1.0 restoration — a seniors' legacy single-seater EV left half-finished. Auditing original schematics, rerouting the wiring harness, and getting the car to a drivable state.
  • Auditing the original schematic diagrams and wiring harness documentation left by the previous team.
  • Rerouting the loom for cleaner grounds, shorter runs, and safer HV terminations.
  • Coordinating across mechanical and electrical subteams alongside the club VP to complete the build.
JUN · SEP 2024

DNDTS

Freelance Repair Engineer
Diagnosed and repaired consumer electronics through full disassembly, component-level replacement, and reassembly.
  • Resolved complex hardware defects including screen & battery replacements.
  • Managed spare-parts inventory and trained new team members in careful handling of delicate components.
OCT 2021 · FEB 2022

Rolls-Royce @ NTU Corporate Lab

Research Student Intern · Aerospace R&D
Designed and fabricated an 8-layer PCB prototype built around MOSFETs in TO-247-4 packages with ceramic capacitor filter banks, for an aerospace R&D programme. Modelled the MOSFET 3D geometry in CAD to verify mechanical fit before committing to board layout.
  • Evaluated alternative components on cost/performance, recommending options that trimmed projected BOM cost by up to 10%.
  • Produced CAD models of aerospace electronic components for mechanical integration and fit verification.
  • Ran bi-weekly reviews with a multidisciplinary research team to keep the board layout on track.
